summary: Solid early‑89 show with strong second set and debut of new songs
blurb: The band opens with the fresh “Touch of Grey” suite and rolls into a high‑energy second set highlighted by “Playing In The Band,” “He’s Gone,” and an extended “Morning Dew.” Standout moments include the debut of “We Can Run/Standing On The Moon” and a powerful vocal/guitar performance from Jerry.
review:
Feb 5, 1989 at the Henry J. Kaiser Convention Center captures the Dead in a transitional phase – new material is still finding its footing while the classic fire of the late‑‘80s shines through. The first set feels a little uneven; “Touch of Grey” → “Feel Like A Stranger” → “Franklin’s Tower” is crisp but some listeners note a flat tone on “Touch.” However, the band quickly regains momentum with a confident “Althea” and the debut of “We Can Run,” followed by a gritty “Desolation Row.”

The second set is where the night truly lifts. “Cold Rain And Snow” launches into a tight “Man Smart, Woman Smarter,” then the brand‑new “Standing On The Moon” lands with gusto. A soaring “Playing In The Band” jam feeds into an inspired drum/space segment before Jerry’s emotive vocal and lead work on “He’s Gone.” The set closes with “I Need A Miracle” and a slow‑burning, two‑chorus “Morning Dew,” which many reviewers call a highlight of the year. An encore of “Johnny B. Goode” caps off a show that, despite some patchy pacing, delivers several must‑hear moments for any Deadhead.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Multiple recordings (2 positive, 4 mixed) praise high‑energy moments, standout jams and the debut of new songs, while only one recording is outright negative. The balance leans positively, yielding a modest +0.8 sentiment boost.

best_recording.reason: Excellent soundboard capture with crystal‑clear balance, no dropouts, and praised for highlighting the extended “Morning Dew” solo and overall full‑show completeness.

key_highlights: Debut of “We Can Run” and “Standing On The Moon” in the first set
Powerful second‑set flow featuring “Playing In The Band,” “He’s Gone,” and an extended, two‑chorus “Morning Dew”
High‑energy opening five songs and strong vocal/guitar work from Jerry on “Althea” and “I Need A Miracle"

band_performance.Jerry: Delivers emotive vocals and a standout lead on “Althea” and the post‑space jam of “He’s Gone,” with shredding runs through the Touch → Stranger suite, though his tone on the opening “Touch of Grey” is noted as a bit flat.
band_performance.Phil: Provides a solid, loud bass foundation; praised for driving the “Playin'” jam and maintaining steady rhythm throughout both sets.
band_performance.Brent: Mixed reception – lauded for dramatic organ on “Morning Dew” and energetic playing on new material, but early‑set tunes like “We Can Run" are described as awkward or overly wordy by some listeners.
band_performance.Mickey: Shows moments of brilliance, especially the “slicing and dicing” during the second set, yet several reviews cite a lack of sync with Billy in parts of the show.
band_performance.Billy: Provides reliable rock‑solid drumming on many tracks, though occasional unevenness with Mickey is mentioned.
